item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doctor probably wished they could find a way to save the human form but continued containment was more important!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as I can tell, the general trend seems to be that of fluctuating size. The quote you included even states "size at birth," which can be, and often is, many times smaller than the body of a fully grown individual of another animal.

Marsupials could also be invoked to solve a shrinking problem, as marsupial infants are many, many times smaller than their fully grown counterparts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The form taken by SCP-1267-1 thus far appears to be constrained to template species whose size at birth is no greater than the dimensions of SCP-1267 itself.

Many mammals are larger than a squirrel at birth. In fact, overall the animals should be getting smaller and smaller (not always, but that should be the trend long term). Elephants and whales are probably out of the picture by now. The events in the article are the opposite of this.

An interesting quirk could have been the containment procedures shifting over time from keeping large animals under control to little critters not getting through small holes.

If you wanted an with some pizzazz, one iteration could produce an unknown or anomalous mammal that is microscopic.

This is to big a contradiction to ignore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This didn't really do anything for me. There's not much there there beyond some mildly disturbing imagery. -1
Congrats :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this, but I kept thinking as I read 'I hope he doesn't go for the obvious oh no it's a human now how awful - because it's awful enough with all the other animals.' But then you did, and it spoilt it hugely for me. Not enough to not upvote, and I guess other people like it, but it felt a bit cheap to me. Things can be horrifying and disgusting without having to involve humans at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this self-reincarnating omni-mammal even without the last log, but that makes it 10 times the SP it was before. It was fabolous as I was suddenly reminded that humans are mammals, too. And that when it's in human form, it remembers its past incarnations… It really adds much to the mystery, and that's what an SCP needs to do!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good and you should feel good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It actually doesn't always go in increasing size. In particular, metamorphosis brought on by trauma almost always results in a small (i.e. fast-growing) SCP-1267-1, but sometimes a normal event will as well. The netting on the enclosure is to prevent the escape of a small organism, and the bi-weekly sonograms are to avoid missing a particularly brief gestation (thanks to research for this SCP, I now know that some mammalian gestation periods are as short as 12-13 days).

In the interest of brevity I cut out a full list of metamorphic events and at least three additional incidents including acquisition (I'm thinking about ditching the cloning experiment as well, since it's quite interesting to me but doesn't do much for the story). There have been many events since the animal arrived in Foundation custody; those mentioned are more of a "highlights reel." The one I really wish was worth including is the reason it's kept at an oceanic facility - a dolphin coming out of a water buffalo. ^_^

Regarding consumption: I view it more as an unsettlingly voracious first meal than "consumed the entire carcass" - mostly to avoid the ridiculous possibility of something like a rabbit eating an elephant. I could clarify it if people think it would improve the article, but I'm really trying to "tell more with less" wherever possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty great. One thing I did notice is that the template species seem to be getting larger over time - squirrel > flying fox > wolf > human > antelope; however, this isn't mentioned in the article, which one would expect if 1267 was on its way to blue whalehood. Given that 'hundreds' of metamorphoses are mentioned I'm presuming this is just a fluke - perhaps to avoid this effect wolf and flying fox could be switched around?

Edit: Actually, scrap that. You need something big enough to plausibly birth a human child, though I do note that means you've currently got a human baby consuming a full-grown female wolf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very simple concept, executed neatly. I was expecting a human and got one, and I think it works just fine. Not bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose the execution could be a bit better, but with an idea this good and an ending that I particularly like, it's not bad enough to interfere with my enjoyment of it. I really like how, in the collapsible, the one doctor is the only one freaked out by 1267's humanity and awareness — the other doctor (and presumably the Foundation as a whole) are like "yeah, been there, done that".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The language is slightly murky, and the ending squick feels cheap. I like it, but not enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I now realize this. It was late when I first read this, and this was before the author made several revisions. Upon initial reading, I mistakenly thought that this SCP was just an antelope that made more antelope that made more antelope, which is why I reacted the way I did. I now know better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was a previous instance of SCP-1267. Maybe even right before it became an antelope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. The tone's slightly iffy at parts, but other than that, it's fairly well written. The idea's original, I think, and that final bit made me cringe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're quite welcome. That fixed it (for me, anyway).

Of course, now the part of my brain that likes to keep me up at night is trying to imagine what a 12-year-old girl trapped in a horrorshow existence like that might draw…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hrm? I think the point is that ONE of its iterations was (and may again be) a human, not that it 'always was'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The execution isn't very good. I get that you're trying to spoiler that it's a human, but this is the Foundation. We're more specific than you're being, and being vague shatters suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. People tend to forget that humans, except for a few pieces of brain tissue, are biologically similar to most mammals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only the final bit saved you from a downvote from me. The rest was uninteresting at best. I do like people being sad by someone getting their guts ripped open.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing that out. I tweaked the interview slightly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially going to downvote because I don't understand how she'd be able to communicate the fact that she remembers all the previous transformations unless she was "born" able to speak English, which wouldn't make any sense. Then it occurred to me that what's really horrifying here is not that she's going to be ripped apart by whatever hatches from her, but that she knows it's going to happen.

Dear god.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the idea of metamorphosis in a group of organisms that generally don't undergo metamorphosis. I liked the visceral and somewhat gory cycle, especially the cannabalistic touch.

But it was the interview that sealed the deal. I don't think it needs any clarification at all; the sweet horror at realizing what "we all knew it could happen, eventually" means was excellent. And that she remembers? And that it's a pronghorn now so that means the Foundation probably did nothing to help her when she was literally being torn apart from the inside? Just the right amount of detail there and each bit keeps adding.

Overall it is a tad long. I liked the bit about moving to an oceanic facility, but some of the other containment details could be omitted or condensed - the observation walkway and some of the detail about the vets, for example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, when you showed me the original draft, I wasn't sure how soon you were gonna post this (sometimes just taking the leap is the fastest way to learn, though)— so I'll reiterate that it definitely needs more of a stylistic/grammar razor. Just take it sentence by sentence and simplify. It's far from incomprehensible, but it is definitely murky.

The final addendum is iffy, too, dialogue-wise… although I don't see why the collapsible formatting is a problem.

Issues aside, though, I do really like the cyclical zoological horror of this concept (even without the slightly sensational ending).

Edit: aaand some of those edits already make a significant difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the changes. Downvote changed to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If a person misses the point the humans are mammalian……

really, the first thing i thought of was 'okay, where is the human thing going to…. oh there it is'

but i can see how it a little unclear

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All that part needs is clarification, really. I mean, I did notice that the manifestation within the SCP was mammilian, but I mean, it's an antelope. Without anything poining out the possibility that it could create a human,it just comes (almost) too suddenly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm guessing that the human child was a previous metamorphosis of SCP-1267, before it was the antelope, perhaps the antelope was the next metamorphosis.

a bit of clarification would fix it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't want to just come out and say it (regarding the child). I could change it to "It'll rip her in half when it comes out."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im up-voting this on faith because of the under-current of horror. I think you should address the question of where the human offspring came from, but I like the potential for this idea very much

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: O4 changed to Level 4.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. I know there's no canon, but, ah, I…cannot say that I have ever seen the term "O4" before. It's not really a clearance level (as far as I know), as O5 simply means Overseer.
  2. Where the hell did that apparently human child come from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback; I've tweaked a few things.
- got rid of all but one collapsible
- ditched the latin
- simplified some of the description
- staff are now Level 2 & 4

Unfortunately, chat wasn't very responsive to my requests for feedback while it was still in sandbox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• "Class-2" and "Class-4" are inconsistent with Foundation personnel classification. The containment procedures are also entirely too long.
  • Even in peer-reviewed scientific journals, you will see the common name of an animal in parentheses after the scientific name. I shouldn't have to Google Latin just to know you're talking about an antelope.
  • The Description is so choked full of technobabble and unnecessarily complicated language that I have absolutely no idea what your SCP does.
  • The collapsibles are extremely annoying. The article isn't long enough to merit them and they are fantastically annoying to a reader since clicking on them breaks your reading flow.

You have been around chat long enough to know that you should have sandboxed this and gotten feedback beforehand. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello! I'm pretty happy with how this turned out, but do have two unresolved concerns I'll put right out in front in the hopes of feedback from more experienced authors.
1) It feels a little long. I'd be happy to shorten it but I'm not sure where.
2) I'm torn on whether to include a photo, and if so, what. Two options in my mind are <picture of SCP-1267> (boring) or a picture of the aftermath of a metamorphosis (too unnecessarily gory?).

Thanks!
